family_audit.reason: Round 15 only moved runtime from 24.17859173 ms to 24.17100811 ms, but it also nudged barrier from 5.48% to 5.21% and long-scoreboard from 7.16% to 7.08% on the same PTX winner. That is not enough to promote the branch to accepted base, but it is enough to justify one more bounded control-path exploit on the live PTX surface.
The current source is already on the restored PTX winner surface, so the restore family is absorbed and should not consume another round right now.
The export-cleanup family is already baked into the present PTX surface and should stay filtered out unless a later source diff shows drift.
This remains the best alternate PTX state recovered from round_history and should stay in the live queue as the first fallback if the active grouped_rows=4 control family stalls.
This preserves one orthogonal geometry-side escape hatch from round_history so the queue does not collapse into only PTX-local variants.

directions.name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit
directions.hypothesis: Round 15 kept the restored PTX microkernel on top and produced a small but real scheduler-facing nudge in the right direction: runtime improved from 24.17859173 ms to 24.17100811 ms, barrier eased from 5.48% to 5.21%, and long-scoreboard eased from 7.16% to 7.08%, while active warps stayed pinned around 16.6%. That is too small to replace the accepted base and still leaves the branch about 0.0067 ms behind the 24.16427231 ms best-known run, so the family should be treated as a flat-positive continuation rather than a closed win. The best next move is therefore one more bounded PTX control-path exploit on the current winner surface, explicitly avoiding the already measured B-first prologue, issue-grouping replay, A-then-B handoff refill, and pure future_tile_k hoist variants.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual cp.async handoff and inner-loop control overhead in the active 128x128 PTX hot-band microkernel, not DRAM bandwidth saturation and not another restore-only action.
directions.risk: Moderate. The family is still live and slightly positive, but another tweak must stay narrowly distinct from the already closed control-order, handoff-order, and address-hoist sub-variants.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.178592 ms accepted base and the 24.164272 ms best-known run
ranking_notes: The latest measured run is effectively a flat-positive result on the PTX winner surface rather than a clean family closeout.
This remains the highest-priority live frontier family once absorbed restore/export families and the closed handoff replay are filtered out.
It ranks first because it stays on the measured best surface while still targeting the only counters that moved in the right direction last round.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the next implementation replays a previously measured prologue, handoff, issue-grouping, or pure address-hoist sub-variant, or if another bounded control tweak stays runtime-flat without improving the scheduler signature.

directions.name: Restore accepted grouped_rows=8 hot-band consumer ordering
directions.hypothesis: The user explicitly asked to repopulate the live search queue from promising round_history states after the search-policy reset, and the grouped_rows=8 PTX consumer-ordering surface remains the strongest alternate PTX representative in that recovered queue. Round 15 did not fail, but it also did not graduate into a new accepted base, which means the primary grouped_rows=4 PTX branch is still live without being decisively finished. That keeps grouped_rows=8 as the best second-ranked fallback: it preserves the PTX microkernel path while materially changing CTA grouping and consumer locality, so it remains the cleanest alternate PTX state to revisit if the active control-path exploit family stalls again.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Grouped-row traversal and consumer-order locality in the PTX hot-band path, not the same steady-state wait/commit seam targeted by dir_01.
directions.risk: Moderate. This is a restore onto a historically promising but currently non-default PTX surface, so it is a fallback rather than the first action while the grouped_rows=4 branch is still giving small positive signal.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.178592 ms accepted base and the 24.164272 ms best-known run
correctness pass rate across all 3 cases
ranking_notes: This stays second because it is the best recovered alternate PTX state from round_history after the queue-family contraction.
It ranks behind dir_01 because the current grouped_rows=4 PTX surface is still the measured leader and the latest control-path branch did not turn negative.
Keeping it live satisfies the queue-repopulation requirement without prematurely abandoning the active winner branch.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the grouped_rows=8 restore remains materially behind the grouped_rows=4 PTX winner without producing a cleaner barrier or scoreboard signature.

directions.name: Promote The Existing 256x128 Pivot Hot-Band Kernel
directions.hypothesis: The live queue still needs one geometry-side branch that does not merely reshuffle the current PTX 128x128 control path. The in-tree 256x128 hot-band kernel already exists with async staging and higher reuse, and round_history kept it among the stronger non-PTX family representatives. Promoting that kernel as the hot-band default would test whether a larger reuse tile can reduce control overhead and improve latency hiding more effectively than another local PTX micro-scheduling change. It remains third because the active PTX branch is still the measured winner and grouped_rows=8 is the stronger like-for-like fallback inside the PTX family.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Geometry-level latency hiding and control amortization on the 256x128 hot-band path, not the current 128x128 PTX wait/commit seam.
directions.risk: Medium to high. This is a broader hot-band dispatch swap onto a non-default geometry, so it is reversible but carries more surface-change risk than the two PTX-local options above.
ranking_notes: This keeps one orthogonal geometry branch live after the search-policy reset so the queue does not collapse into only PTX-local variants.
It ranks third because the measured best surface is still the 128x128 PTX branch and grouped_rows=8 is the cleaner within-family fallback.
The upside is broader than dir_01 and dir_02, but so is the implementation surface, which is why it stays below the two PTX options.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the 256x128 promotion cannot get materially closer to the 24.16-24.18 ms PTX band or if it opens a broad hot-band regression without counter improvements.

notes: Round 16 treats the latest PTX control-path result as a flat-positive continuation rather than a decisive closeout: the branch improved the accepted-base runtime slightly but not enough to replace the best-known 24.164272 ms run or to promote the accepted base in search state. The absorbed restore/export-cleanup families stay filtered out on the current source, while grouped_rows=8 and the 256x128 pivot branch remain explicitly live because the user asked to repopulate the frontier from promising round_history states after the search-policy update.
